dailyJudgementSnippet: Google Gemini narrowly won the day by being the least negative overall, though this was more a case of attracting limited, mostly neutral discussion than clear enthusiasm: its small set of mentions still included quality complaints and hallucination criticism [E9][E10][E11], alongside at least one plainly neutral technical reference [E12]. By contrast, OpenAI and Microsoft Copilot drew heavier, more concentrated backlash, while Anthropic faced broad criticism around Claude bugs, pricing, and trust [E5][E6][E7][E8].
winnerExplanation: Google Gemini had the best aggregate sentiment score of the four, but it won a relatively weak field rather than posting genuinely positive sentiment. Its discussion volume was very small compared with OpenAI and Anthropic, and the evidence shows some negative product-quality comments about incorrect or unhelpful answers [E9][E10][E11]. Still, Gemini appears to have benefited from lighter scrutiny overall and some neutral technical discussion [E12], which kept its net sentiment less negative than peers. Anthropic finished behind Gemini because, despite a large volume of mentions, a substantial share centered on Claude regressions, wasted spend, buggy behavior, and complaints about the company’s tone and trustworthiness [E5][E6][E7][E8]. OpenAI scored worse still, with repeated criticism focused on ads, business strategy, lack of trust, and broader reputational decline [E1][E2][E3][E4]. Microsoft Copilot was the clear laggard, driven by strong negative reactions to training on user code, licensing concerns, distrust of Microsoft/GitHub, and complaints about Copilot being pushed into the product experience [E13][E14][E15][E16]. So Gemini won because it was the least disliked, not because Hacker News was especially positive about it.

openai.judgementSnippet: OpenAI had a rough day, with strong criticism aimed at ChatGPT ads, business direction, trust, and company reputation [E1][E2][E3][E4].

anthropic.judgementSnippet: Anthropic saw broad negative discussion around Claude regressions, wasted tokens/cost, buggy behavior, and frustration with the company’s posture and communication [E5][E6][E7][E8].

google_gemini.judgementSnippet: Google Gemini was still net negative, with complaints about wrong or unhelpful answers, but it faced much lighter criticism overall and some neutral discussion, leaving it least negative on the day [E9][E10][E11][E12].

microsoft_copilot.judgementSnippet: Microsoft Copilot fared worst, with concentrated backlash over code-training practices, licensing and trust concerns, and a feeling that Copilot is being forced on users [E13][E14][E15][E16].

evidence.summary: Directly criticizes OpenAI/ChatGPT as disgusting and says the ‘golden age’ is ending with ‘enshitification,’ indicating strong negative sentiment toward OpenAI’s strategy around ChatGPT ads.
Direct criticism of ChatGPT/OpenAI’s ad-heavy business model and company strategy, framed as evidence that AGI does not exist.

The comment says OpenAI is banned and not trusted, feels far behind and playing catch-up, and that its perception is rock-bottom due to Sam Altman. This is clearly negative toward OpenAI as a company/provider.
Direct criticism of OpenAI’s company strategy, describing it as becoming a commoditized 'dumb pipe'.

The post describes Claude Managed Agents repeatedly appending a malware-check prompt, wasting time/tokens, refusing to proceed, and charging for each failed session. It explicitly asks Anthropic to fix the regression to stop users losing money.
The comment is sharply critical of Anthropic’s strategy, pricing burden, self-righteous brand, and buggy software, calling it unbearable and saying they miss OpenAI's style by comparison.

The commenter calls Anthropic an "entirely unserious company" and says they dumped Claude Code after being "gaslit" and "lied to" about service degrades. This is a direct strong negative judgment of Anthropic and its Claude Code handling.
Direct criticism of Claude Code’s review/acceptance and prompt design; says the prompt was 'very poorly phrased' and that it should obviously have prevented the agent from making changes.
The comment describes Google search previews as confidently giving an incorrect answer about the whale name, framing it as part of a broader comparison with other search-enabled LLMs.
The commenter says Gemini returns multiple incorrect champions and irrelevant event details, implying poor answer quality and hallucination on this query.
The commenter says Gemini gave unhelpful, nonsensical guidance when asked for help, implying frustration with the model’s quality or usefulness.

The comment uses Gemma 4 only as an illustrative example while explaining inference bottlenecks and memory bandwidth. It does not praise or criticize Google Gemini/Gemma itself, nor compare it against other models.

The commenter says they stopped serious work on GitHub after learning CoPilot was trained on everyone's code without opt-in and with unclear license handling, which is a strong negative judgment of Microsoft Copilot/GitHub Copilot's company strategy and data use.

The comment blames Copilot for deepening distrust in GitHub/Microsoft, cites concerns about training on public/GPL repos, and says Copilot entrenched Microsoft’s development philosophy in a negative way.

The comment blames GitHub's decline on 'AI agents' and Microsoft ownership, framing the AI takeover as harmful and saying it 'ran it into the ground.' This is an implied but clearly negative judgment that can reasonably extend to Microsoft Copilot as part of Microsoft's AI push.

The comment says Microsoft is forcing Copilot on users and that it is annoyingly present by default throughout GitHub, which is direct criticism of the Copilot product experience and rollout strategy.
